request.question: Are the Films Nitrate Kisses and Italy in a Day documentaries?

title: Love and Kisses from Brotherhood of Man
George Eastman House Motion Picture Collection
Lyrical Nitrate
Nitrate Kisses
Italy in a Day
Santa María School massacre
Tender Fictions
Perdón, viejita
Chacabuco
Vampire Kisses
sentences: Love and Kisses from Brotherhood of Man (usually referred to as simply Love and Kisses) is the second album released by the new line-up of British pop group Brotherhood of Man.
It was released in the UK the day before their 1976 Eurovision win with "Save Your Kisses for Me", which the album contained.
In some European countries the album was released in October 1975 with a slightly different track listing.
The George Eastman Museum Motion Picture Collection in Rochester, New York comprises about 28,000 titles, including features, shorts, documentaries, newsreels, and experimental moving images.
The collection is renowned for its holdings of silent films.
The George Eastman Museum owns the Louis B. Mayer Conservation Center, where its holdings of nitrate films are stored.
In 1996, the Eastman Museum founded the L. Jeffrey Selznick School of Film Preservation.
Lyrical Nitrate (Dutch: "Lyrisch Nitraat" ) is a 1991 collage film by Peter Delpeut.
The film consists of clips from various silent films printed on decaying nitrocellulose (or nitrate) film stock, including shorts, documentaries, and travelogues.
There is no formal narrative.
Delpeut followed the film with 1993's "The Forbidden Quest", which also uses found footage; the two were released together on video and DVD.
Nitrate Kisses is a 1992 experimental documentary film directed by Barbara Hammer.
According to Hammer, it is an exploration of the repression and marginalization of LGBT people since the First World War.
To celebrate the 30th anniversary of the Teddy Awards, the film has been selected to be shown at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival in February 2016.
Italy in a Day - Un giorno da italiani is a 2014 Italian-British documentary film directed by Gabriele Salvatores.
It was part of the Out of Competition section at the 71st Venice International Film Festival.

The Santa María School massacre was a massacre of striking workers, mostly saltpeter works (nitrate) miners, along with wives and children, committed by the Chilean Army in Iquique, Chile on December 21, 1907.
The number of victims is undetermined but is reliably estimated at over 2,000.
It occurred during the peak of the nitrate mining era, which coincided with the Parliamentary Period in Chilean political history (1891–1925).
With the massacre and an ensuing reign of terror, not only was the strike broken, but the workers' movement was thrown into limbo for over a decade.

For decades afterward there was official suppression of knowledge of the incident, but in 2007 the government conducted a highly publicized commemoration of its centenary, including an official national day of mourning and the reinterment of the victims' remains.
Tender Fictions is a 1996 autobiographical documentary film directed by American experimental filmmaker Barbara Hammer.
It is the second of a trilogy of documentary films that includes "Nitrate Kisses" and "History Lessons".
Together, the three films are sometimes known as the "History trilogy".
"Tender Fictions" details Hammer's life and her attempts to "construct" a self.
The film was nominated for a prize at the 1996 Sundance Film Festival.
Perdón, viejita is a 1927 silent Argentine film directed and written by José A. Ferreyra.
Ferreyra was born on August 28, 1889 in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
He was a director and writer also known for Bewitching Kisses (1937) and La vuelta al Bulín (1926).
Ferreyra died on January 29, 1943, at age 53.
A fragment (34-minutes) of the film is currently available on YouTube.
The restored version will be presented in the San Isidro Film and Music Festival on November 15.
The nitrate copy of the film was provided by the Fundación Cinemateca Argentina and the restoration work was done by GOTIKA,
Chacabuco is one of the many abandoned nitrate or "saltpeter" towns ("oficinas salitreras" in Spanish) in the Atacama Desert of northern Chile.
Other nitrate towns of the Atacama Desert include Humberstone and Santa Laura Saltpeter Works.
Unlike most of the other ghost towns in the Atacama Desert, Chacabuco became a concentration camp during the Pinochet regime in 1973.
To this day, it remains surrounded by approximately 98 lost landmines, left by the Chilean military when Chacabuco was used as a prison camp.
Vampire Kisses is a series of books written by Ellen Schreiber.
Vampire Kisses is about a 16-year-old girl named Raven Madison who is a goth misfit in her polo-wearing, ordinary, town.
When an old abandoned mansion finally gets new residents, the rumors start to spread.
Everyone in the small town, which Raven refers to as “Dullsville”, believes that the new neighbors are actually secret bloodthirsty vampires.
Even Raven, who has always loved vampires since she was little, believes the rumors.
But one day, she encounters the attractive yet mysterious Alexander Sterling that lives in the mansion, and feels like he is the only person that actually understands her.
The two very quickly fall in love, but still the question remains; are the Sterlings really vampires?

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Are the Films Nitrate Kisses and Italy in a Day documentaries?
hop1_docs: Nitrate Kisses | Nitrate Kisses is a 1992 experimental documentary film directed by Barbara Hammer.  According to Hammer, it is an exploration of the repression and marginalization of LGBT people since the First World War.  To celebrate the 30th anniversary of the Teddy Awards, the film has been selected to be shown at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival in February 2016.

Tender Fictions | Tender Fictions is a 1996 autobiographical documentary film directed by American experimental filmmaker Barbara Hammer.  It is the second of a trilogy of documentary films that includes "Nitrate Kisses" and "History Lessons".  Together, the three films are sometimes known as the "History trilogy".  "Tender Fictions" details Hammer's life and her attempts to "construct" a self.  The film was nominated for a prize at the 1996 Sundance Film Festival.

Lyrical Nitrate | Lyrical Nitrate (Dutch: "Lyrisch Nitraat" ) is a 1991 collage film by Peter Delpeut.  The film consists of clips from various silent films printed on decaying nitrocellulose (or nitrate) film stock, including shorts, documentaries, and travelogues.  There is no formal narrative.  Delpeut followed the film with 1993's "The Forbidden Quest", which also uses found footage; the two were released together on video and DVD.

Italy in a Day | Italy in a Day - Un giorno da italiani is a 2014 Italian-British documentary film directed by Gabriele Salvatores.  It was part of the Out of Competition section at the 71st Venice International Film Festival.

Kiss Loves You | KISS Loves You is a 2004 documentary film directed by Jim Heneghan and co-produced by 8th Grade Films and Sveriges Television.  The documentary chronicles the triumphs, tragedies and absurdities of a select group of KISS fans over a ten-year period beginning in 1994, during which the band reunited in original greasepaint and costumes.

The Gates of Italy | The Gates of Italy is a 21-minute 1943 Canadian documentary film, made by the National Film Board of Canada (NFB) as part of both the wartime "Canada Carries On" and "The World in Action" series.  The film was produced by Stuart Legg and Tom Daly.  "The Gates of Italy" describes the last days of Benito Mussolini's rule over Italy in 1943 during the Second World War.

Kissing on the Mouth | Kissing on the Mouth is a 2005 American film directed by Joe Swanberg.  The small cast served as the only crew, with the film featuring real interviews with recent college graduates and a documentary approach to the graphic sex and conversations.  It is considered one of the original films of the Mumblecore movement.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Documentary status of the films "Nitrate Kisses" and "Italy in a Day"

- Passage [1]: "Nitrate Kisses is a 1992 experimental documentary film directed by Barbara Hammer."  
- Passage [4]: "Italy in a Day - Un giorno da italiani is a 2014 Italian-British documentary film directed by Gabriele Salvatores."  
- Ambiguity / missing facts: None — both passages explicitly label each film as a "documentary film." No additional retrieval needed.  
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority): 1) No additional retrieval needed. 2) (If more detail desired) "Nitrate Kisses" — full article (lede/infobox, "Synopsis"/"Reception"). 3) (If more detail desired) "Italy in a Day" — full article (lede/infobox, "Synopsis"/"Reception").
response.hop2_query: Nitrate Kisses full article lede infobox
hop2_docs: Infobox | An infobox is a template used to collect and present a subset of information about its subject, such as a document.  It is a structured document containing a set of attribute–value pairs, and in Wikipedia represents a summary of information about the subject of an article.  In this way, they are comparable to data tables in some aspects.  When presented within the larger document it summarizes, an infobox is often presented in a sidebar format.

Nitrate Kisses | Nitrate Kisses is a 1992 experimental documentary film directed by Barbara Hammer.  According to Hammer, it is an exploration of the repression and marginalization of LGBT people since the First World War.  To celebrate the 30th anniversary of the Teddy Awards, the film has been selected to be shown at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival in February 2016.
List of lakes in Wyoming | "Information listed is taken from the infobox in the associated article unless otherwise specified."
Marquess of Lede | The Marquess of Lede (Spanish: Marquesado de Lede) was a Flemish title in use during the Ancien Regime, Lede is a city in Flanders.

Lede Formation | The Lede Formation (Dutch: "Formatie van Lede" ; abbreviation: Ld) is a geologic formation in the subsurface of Belgium.  The formation is named after the town of Lede in East Flanders.  It consists of shallow-marine limestone and sandstone, deposited in the former sea that covered Belgium during the Eocene.

LEDE | The Linux Embedded Development Environment (LEDE) project is founded as a fork of the OpenWrt project and shares many of the same goals.  It was created in May 2016 by a group of core OpenWrt contributors due to disagreements on OpenWrt internal processes.  The schism was nominally reconciled a year later in May 2017 pending approval of the LEDE developers.  The proposed remerger will preserve the OpenWrt branding but use many of the LEDE processes and rules.  The remerge proposal vote was passed by LEDE developers in June 2017.

Lead paragraph | In writing, especially in journalism, a lead paragraph (sometimes shortened to lead; also spelled lede) is the opening paragraph of an article, essay, book chapter, or other written work that summarizes its main ideas.
response.summary_2: Nitrate Kisses is a 1992 experimental documentary film directed by Barbara Hammer, and Italy in a Day - Un giorno da italiani is a 2014 Italian-British documentary film directed by Gabriele Salvatores. Nitrate Kisses explores the repression and marginalization of LGBT people since the First World War and was selected to screen at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival for the Teddy Awards' 30th anniversary.
